speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is tape five of the interview with Jacob Rosenberg. Jacob, you were telling us about the camp in the [NON-ENGLISH] mountains in the forest. You said that this was the most horrible camp. Can you tell us why?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way-- well, first of all, because of the climatic conditions. Even in-- even in spring, there was winter there. It was constantly wet. The ground was wet because the sun did not penetrate [INAUDIBLE] time when I was there. And the work was horrible. I worked for a firm called [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and also, again, in transport. We didn't get any food. The food was practically nothing. We-- we were starving. And the beatings, the beatings were constantly. I can't remember any camp getting so many. For the slightest thing, you were beaten up. And hangings were just one of the daily pastimes of the Germans. Hang-- hang a person, it was nothing. Just put the-- a rope up on-- on-- on a branch of a tree, and hang him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you maintain your courage to stay alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Good question-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you were working in the transport. What what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, dragging concrete slabs to build the underground factories, where they hoped to be able to produce the V-2 and V-3, you know, those flying bombs. And the-- the factories were so deep underground, no bomb could destroy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In barracks-- there were barracks. We had three bunks-- one, two, three, you know. I-- I slept on the top bunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you receive a number in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not there, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- we arrived there in January. I think January, or perhaps-- yeah, January till May. 8Th of May, I was liberated the 8th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard news about-- that the war was coming to an end before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we knew that the war was coming to an end.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the news came in. There was also a political segment of inmates in camp. And they received. There were Germans, French, Dutch, also Poles. And-- and they-- they had news from-- from outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at liberation? What happened on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: A horrible day, a horrible day, we had nowhere to go. I'm talking about Jewish people. We had no home. We had no one left. There was no one waiting for us. The other nationalities were greeting the Americans with their own flags. Poles had their flag, French, and so on. We had no flag. The Polish citizens, the Poles wouldn't let the Polish citizens stand with them. The Yiddish, the Jewish citizens, they wouldn't let them stand with them. They would-- they would chase us away. The Hungarians had no affinity with the Hungarian prisoners. So we were really the pariahs of the pariahs. But something very-- very human did occur. A group of American soldiers came behind the tanks. There worked behind the tanks. And they asked if there are any Jews in camp. And they were pointed out that we are the Jews. There are a group of Jews standing there. And they were also Jewish people from New York, American Jews. For the first time, we've seen men, Jewish people, with Tommy guns, with armor, with tanks. We didn't believe things like this exist. And it was-- this was-- --a reunion which I'll never forget. And although I was-- after the war, I became very hardened. I've seen a lot of dead, a lot of murder. And for the first time in six years, I cried like a little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we-- we left the camp. I established myself with a group of boys somewhere in a-- in a little barrack, another barrack. And we had a-- there was a chance for us to go to Israel. And I-- I guess there was no-- there was such a chaos. You didn't have any tickets. You didn't even-- all those formalities, papers. You just took a train. We were-- we finished up Italy. First in Salzburg, and then to Italy, and there I met my wife, Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't return to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I had no reason to return to Lodz, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult crossing the border into Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't want to back to Lodz. Perhaps most probably because I was with a lot of Polish people in camp. And they gave me a taste of the-- of brotherhood, so-- so to speak. I still haven't gone back because I knew that no one survived. I had no one waiting for me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to arrive in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a-- a day, we just crossed the border, you know. It's not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a camp, displaced person camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Santa Maria di Bagni. And there I met, as I told you, I met Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about your first meeting with Esther?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, actually, I met her in the evening. And I was reciting one of my poems. And it was a [NON-ENGLISH], a wedding. And I was reciting one of my poems, and I invited Esther to dance. And that was a bad dance, and she left me on the floor. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you convince her to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somehow, she thought maybe-- I don't know-- I-- I-- you have to ask her. But I remember that we took to one another. We found a-- a something in common, which I can not recollect. I don't know how to explain. And we feel for one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved her from the moment I've seen her. And because we were lonely, there was no one to ask. Can we get married? No, we-- we made the decision ourselves. We got married then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I didn't have enough money to pay my-- my fare for a marriage license. So we hired a donkey. And we went with a donkey [INAUDIBLE] little.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946, on 10th of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to express love again after having become hardened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, life [INAUDIBLE] has its ways. And perhaps the-- the strength of hope and of rebuilding is part of our heritage. We didn't become wild. I can't remember Jewish people murdering anybody else after the war, taking revenge. I-- I can't remember those things. They didn't took place. We just were looking for a way to begin again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make sense out of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: To make sense out of such a senseless thing is a great art. And perhaps this is one of those inborn or natural phenomena like you see in winter or in-- or in autumn, a flower wilting away, a plant. The spring revives it again. Perhaps this is the same process takes-- is with people. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you support yourself in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were supported by the-- by American Joint. And there-- but later on, Esther became a librarian. She worked in a library. And I was a-- a script writer for-- for theatre created. It didn't drive us into money, but can have a small support and the-- and the sense of being human again.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language did you write those scripts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we wanted to run away from Europe. And we wanted really to go to Israel. And Esther was already-- she had a certificate to go. But then, they came to her and said, the-- the authorities, those who were in charge of-- of-- of [INAUDIBLE], came to her and said, look, Esther, there's a little boy and a little girl, sister and brother. He got a certificate. She hasn't got. And we are against now dividing the family. We have enough divisions. We want you to give up the certificate. And she did. It most probably was destiny. So she-- otherwise, I wouldn't have met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so did you decide on Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we wanted to get away, yes, wherever we could. And Australia became available. There was, on the horizon, and we decided we'd go. And I think there was something. There was a lot of problems in Europe with the Berlin Wall, with the Cold War. And I said, let's go. So here we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: By ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took me eight weeks. The ship broke down on the way. Most probably they-- they didn't realize they have such precious cargo.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And where did the ship come into port?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fremantle. And we were warmly greeted by the Australian Jews. It was such a warm greeting, such a-- such a brotherhood, such a great feeling, embrace, that our people care for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it to feel such warmth again from people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just incredible. It was just incredible. And I could not understand, and not me and not Esther could understand the sense of freedom that was prevalent in Australia. We never experienced it. We remember a policeman knocking at the door. And he asked permission to just-- to come in. Now, this was not-- not [INAUDIBLE]. We just never heard of a damn policeman to ask your permission. He pushed you away and walked in in Europe. But here, it was-- it was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain in Fremantle?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- oh, I can't remember. Maybe a day or two, or maybe even not that, can't remember. Came to Melbourne from Fremantle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you build up your life again in Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we started working. My wife had no profession. I was also not greatly-- and we only had to go to a factory where they speak Yiddish in order to understand. Imagine when we came here, penniless, speechless, no language, no family, no-- no possessions, and no home. But we put our strength together, I and Esther, and we-- we build a life. We build a life in determination and hope, and-- yeah. And-- and perhaps past, well, what happened was also sort of gave us impetus to not to waste any time, but build life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- my daughter was born in 1953, because we decided we wouldn't like my daughter-- bring a child in a camp. You know, we've been constantly in camps, even in-- in-- in a displaced person camp, but it was a camp. And my Marcia was born here in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been in Australia then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been in Australia about four years or five years-- 1948, 5 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to people a lot about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: We constantly talk about the experience, constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you talk to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly to-- to survivors, mostly to survivors, they understand better than anybody else. They understand the nonspoken word. There is a language which is behind, beyond words they understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet many people from Lodz in Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I met a number of people from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: People that you had known before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there's a number of people I knew before the war. In-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect do you think your experiences have had on the way you brought up your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no special school of bringing up children. You improvise. And my daughter knows everything about our life, about my wife's and about mine. We never-- at the time when she was little, most probably didn't tell her. But later on, we didn't hide anything from her. There's no point in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have dreams about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got constantly dreams. My wife has dreams to [INAUDIBLE]. I remember my wife used to get panicky. And she was wet from-- from-- from fear. She was constantly chased when she went to the Warsaw ghetto. She was in the uprising, and then eight camps afterwards. It's not-- not something that she-- that you can wipe out or forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do when you would have dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] talk to one another. This was like a self-therapy. We didn't go to psychotherapists. Nowadays, something happened, they need counseling. They need-- I don't know. I don't know. For every little thing, they need counseling. We never went to any searching medical help. We talked to one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: What impact did your writing have on your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was your writing-- how did it help you to assimilate your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like anybody else, like any other writer, he writes constantly about himself. No matter what he writes, he's there. And all my writing is most probably also in that sense a constant pilgrimage into the past, but yet with a-- with a view to the future. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have there been any decisions that you've made or things that you've done since coming to Australia that have been influenced by your Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get the question. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How have your experiences of the Holocaust influenced the way you've lived your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live my life, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said before, there is no prep schools for living. There's no prep schools where they teach you how to live. You just live. You improvise. And most probably, I am a very family-consciousness man. And I did everything, primarily, to-- what I can, what is in my power, to give my family a sense of-- of togetherness [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- and belonging, that we belong, that we are part of something. Of course, it's difficult in a community like us to-- for a creative man to be part of the community, and yet be aware of this [INAUDIBLE], of this insular prejudice, which can take place in communal life in small communities. This is-- this is something one has to keep in mind. But my family, my wife and my daughter, were aware of that. And we tried. We're a very strong family-- community-orientated people, very strong. A community means a lot to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your community here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about Jewish people, Jewishness.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who are you personally-- what does that community mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means that I am not totally uprooted. Perhaps I lost the place where I was born. But in a sense, my roots are in time. There's no-- I have no reason or purpose to go back and see the stones and the houses and the museums, which they erected there for there. I think it's just a waste of time and a waste of energy and of-- of your-- of your spiritualism. My-- I believe in the word. And as I-- as I said, my roots are in time. For me, the written word is more than old museums. The poetry, the Holocaust poetry, is my museum. I walk there, and it's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How have your beliefs changed, your beliefs about the world, changed because of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I cannot see the world changed. I can't see it. I think the world is still the same. We've got to make the most of it. But we cannot-- we cannot live with a sense of total pessimism, of total nihilism. And even as I said to you before, I think I read to you a little poem about-- I said to my children, 'children, my children, don't forget this sorrow. But remember, we [INAUDIBLE] be [INAUDIBLE] that builds a better tomorrow. And even you know that this tomorrow will not come, you're not exempt to believe that it's on the way.' Perhaps in a sense it's a little bit of a kabbalistic. But this is the only way to remain human.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this how you remained human in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think kept you alive? What do you think helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a hard question. I don't know. I don't know. Most probably, I had that-- that against all odds, that I'm going to-- I'm going to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now so long after the war, how do you find your writing changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It hasn't changed a great deal. I still write about-- about the war, about my experiences. But I also am more philosophically attuned to human conflict. And I understand, perhaps better, the mechanics of life [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and human relation. And I try to-- to make them part of-- of my horizon of my thinking and as well as my writing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you write about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I write about my family. There are poems I wrote about-- to my-- to my wife and to my children. And-- and of course, I write a lot about my deceased, my murdered family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you have any grandchildren? Yes, I have three grandchildren, three beautiful [INAUDIBLE]. What are their names

speaker: interviewee
text: Adina, Shoshana-- or Shoshi; she likes Shoshi-- and Ariella.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is 14, 11, and 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you talk to them about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, not yet, but they-- Adina knows. She understands. School, and her mother talks to her. I haven't spoken to her directly. But she knows. There is a way of knowing without-- without words, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the greatest way of understanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to give your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, again, I think we at this-- my generation, nature is almost weeding us out from the landscape of life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And something has to be left for posterity. I really don't care the whole battle about the people have to believe. The world has to believe. I really don't go for that. I think I want-- this is for my people, for my people. They have to-- they have to think about it. The world doesn't owe us a living. And I don't expect the-- the Finns or the-- the Hungarians, the Russians, to mourn or to think about what happened to the Jewish people. I don't-- I don't expect it. But what I expect is from you. And from-- from-- from my-- from posterity, from Jewish people, from the-- from the future generations, they have to remember. It's there. It's their history. It's their life, because I don't believe that the human being is an isolated entity. There's always a path of a development, of reaching out in the future. And my children, and my grandchildren, are my future.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think the Jewish community has been-- what impact do you think the Holocaust has had on the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] No, I don't know. This is hard to say. I don't want to go into this. [INAUDIBLE] I like to stay away. It's a very dangerous question.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. 50 years after the Holocaust has ended, what message would you have for your children, your grandchildren, other Jews, or for the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: My message was in that little poem, not to forget. This is my message. But to believe there is a better tomorrow. This is the only way to stay human. If you don't believe, against all the odds, that there is a better tomorrow, you are-- you cannot be a human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to say that we haven't asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say that I have enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Would you like to have those pictures [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes we will. We will take the pictures in a moment. Jacob, can you tell us who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This with my father when he was a young man. He'd just came back from Siberia. He was sent to stay for participating in rebellion against the tsarist system-- Gershon Rosenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is again my father, just taken before the war in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is a group of leading Bundists in Lodz. My father sits here on the extreme right, as you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my father again here with a group of leading Bundists. He is on the far left.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes this is my sister, Pola. She was here 24 years old. Two years later, she was-- she committed suicide in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Marseille. It's my wife, Esther, and me. We went away to Australia. And this is Esther and me on the way to Australia. The picture was taken in this-- in Marseille just before departure. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Esther with my second grandchild. And her reflection or life, we coming back to life again, new generations.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my daughter, Marcia, and my son-in-law, Michael with their second child, Shoshana, Shoshi. My three granddaughters, Adina, Shoshana, Ariella. Thank you. We just celebrated our golden wedding anniversary, and this is a picture of it. This is the cover of the which will be part of my next book. Two books of poetry telling the story of my life. Thank you Two more books, again, a poetry and short stories where the story of the Holocaust is projected. My fifth book, is-- goes into print soon. Thank you.
